revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:お化け暦]]


'''Obake Goyomi''' (お化け暦, "Ghost Calendar") refers to unauthorized and illicitly published calendars in Japan during the Meiji to Showa periods.{{Sfn|Okada & Akune|1993|pp=353-354}}{{Sfn|Sawamiya|2016|p=106}}{{Sfn|Reki no Kai|1999|p=134}} Following the 1872 (Meiji 5) Calendar Reform Edict, official calendars (官暦, kanreki), such as the {{ill|Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū Calendar|ja|神宮暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Calendar|qq=}} and the Abridged Official Calendar (略本暦, Ryakuhonreki), removed all calendar annotations (暦注, rekichū) indicating auspicious or inauspicious days, labeling them as superstitions.{{Sfn|Watanabe|1994|pp=224-225}}{{Sfn|Okada et al.|2006|pp=69-71}} Additionally, in 1910 (Meiji 43), the government discontinued listing the old lunisolar calendar (旧暦, Kyūreki). In response to these restrictions, illicitly produced "Ghost Calendars" flourished among the common people, who still sought such information.{{Sfn|Reki no Kai|1999|p=134}} These calendars were also simply called "Obake" (おばけ) or "Obake" (オバケ) as shorthand.{{Sfn|Reki no Kai|1999|p=214}}{{Sfn|Okada & Akune|1993|pp=331-332}}{{Sfn|Okada|2009|p=197}}

Due to strict crackdowns and wartime paper shortages, the production of these illicit calendars declined sharply after 1941 (Showa 16).{{Sfn|Reki no Kai|1999|pp=134-135}} After World War II, when restrictions on calendar publications were lifted,{{Sfn|Nakamaki|2017|p=17}}{{Sfn|Reki no Kai|1999|p=116}}{{Sfn|Watanabe|1994|p=219}}{{Sfn|Okada & Akune|1993|pp=354-355}}, the term "Ghost Calendar" disappeared.{{Sfn|Sawamiya|2016|p=106}}{{Sfn|Reki no Kai|1999|p=136}} However, contemporary {{ill|Fortune Calendars|ja|運勢暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fortune Calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Lucky Calendars|ja|開運暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lucky Calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} are considered to be successors of the Ghost Calendar tradition.{{Sfn|Okada & Akune|1993|pp=353-354}}{{Sfn|Reki no Kai|1999|p=134}}

== Characteristics ==
Ghost Calendars were distributed under various names, such as "Min'yō Nikki" (民用日記, "Daily Journal for the Common People"), "Kyūsei Hōi Meiji Nichiyōben" (九星方位明治日用便, "Nine Star Directional Almanac for Meiji"), and "Nōka Binran" (農家便覧, "Farmers' Handbook").{{Sfn|Okada|2009|p=197}} To evade government crackdowns, publishers changed locations annually, and publishers often used false names, making their identities difficult to trace—hence the name "Ghost Calendar."{{Sfn|Reki no Kai|1999|p=214}}{{Sfn|Okada|2009|p=197}}{{Sfn|Nakamaki|2017|p=17}}{{Sfn|Watanabe|1994|pp=147-148}} Many of these false publisher names, such as "Fuji-no-I Tokubei" (藤の井徳兵衛) and "Fukunaga Kahei" (福永嘉兵衛), were designed to sound auspicious.{{Sfn|Okada & Akune|1993|p=354}} 

Ghost Calendars were typically formatted similarly to the Abridged Official Calendar (略本暦, Ryakuhonreki) issued by the Jingu Shrine Office, and some even mimicked the authentication seals used on official calendars.{{Sfn|Reki no Kai|1999|p=134}}{{Sfn|Okada & Akune|1993|p=354}}{{Sfn|Okada|2009|p=198}} 

The contents of these calendars included both the new Gregorian calendar (新暦, Shinreki) and the old lunisolar calendar (旧暦, Kyūreki), along with directional fortune-telling (方位, Hōi) and other traditional annotations.{{Sfn|Nakamaki|2014}} They also included information on holidays, shrine festivals, and annual customs.{{Sfn|Reki no Kai|1999|p=134}}{{Sfn|Okada & Akune|1993|p=354}} 

Ghost Calendars listed superstitions such as {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuyō|qq=}} (six-day fortune cycle), {{ill|Nine Star Ki|ja|九星|1=Nine Stars|2=ja|3=九星|lt=Nine Stars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nine Star Ki|qq=}}, and {{ill|Sanrinbō|ja|三隣亡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanrinbō|1=Sanrinbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11358293}} (an unlucky day for construction).{{Sfn|Nakamaki|2017|p=17}}{{Sfn|Okada et al.|2006|p=71}}{{Sfn|Okada & Akune|1993|p=332}}{{Sfn|Sawamiya|2016|p=107}} These annotations, which gained popularity from the late Edo period to the early Meiji period, were absent from earlier traditional calendars.{{Sfn|Reki no Kai|1999|p=134}}{{Sfn|Okada & Akune|1993|p=354}} 

However, since Ghost Calendars were not supervised by calendrical scholars, their accuracy was often questionable, leading to discrepancies even among different editions.{{Sfn|Reki no Kai|1999|p=134}}{{Sfn|Okada & Akune|1993|p=354}} In particular, inconsistencies often arose in interpretations of the Nine Star system and the Sanrinbō inauspicious days.{{Sfn|Reki no Kai|1999|p=134}}{{Sfn|Okada & Akune|1993|p=354}} 

{{multiple image
 | align     = center
 | direction = horizontal
 | footer    = "Meiji 17 (1884) Farmers' Handbook" (one type of Ghost Calendar)
 | footer_align = center
 | image1    = Nouka benran (2).jpg
 | width1    = 250
 | alt1      = 
 | image2    = Nouka benran (1).jpg
 | width2    = 250
 | alt2      = 
 | image3    = Nouka benran (cover).jpg
 | width3    = 137
 | alt3      = 
}}

== Influence on Modern Calendars ==
After calendar publishing was deregulated, fortune calendars began to reappear, listing old lunar dates and auspicious or inauspicious days such as the {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuyō|qq=}}, {{ill|Nine Star Ki|ja|九星|1=Nine Stars|2=ja|3=九星|lt=Nine Stars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nine Star Ki|qq=}}, and {{ill|Sanrinbō|ja|三隣亡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanrinbō|1=Sanrinbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11358293}}.{{Sfn|Watanabe|1994|p=219}} Even though the use of the old calendar faded after 1955, it is still used for traditional events and in the fishing industry for tide predictions.{{Sfn|Nakamaki|2017|p=14}} The Rokuyō cycle continues to have an impact on weddings and funerals.{{Sfn|Nakamaki|2017|p=15}} 

After World War II, even the former official calendar (Jingu Calendar) began listing old lunar dates and Rokuyō fortunes.{{Sfn|Watanabe|1994|p=219}} Today, various fortune calendars and almanacs are published annually, with their lineage tracing back to the Ghost Calendars.{{Sfn|Okada & Akune|1993|pp=353-354}}{{Sfn|Reki no Kai|1999|p=134}}

== Related Topics ==
* {{ill|Japanese calendar|en|Japanese calendar|qid=Q13788|lt=Japanese Calendars|1=Japanese calendar}}
* {{ill|Meiji Calendar Reform|ja|明治改暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Calendar Reform|1=Meiji calendar reform|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125315161}}
* {{ill|Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū Calendar|ja|神宮暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Calendar|qq=}}
* {{ill|Chikuzen Takeyari Rebellion|ja|筑前竹槍一揆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikuzen Takeyari Rebellion|qq=}} - A rebellion demanding the restoration of the old calendar

2025 ==
